title_en: The Fruits of Correct Iman and Its Effect on Life
title_ar: ثمرات الإيمان الصحيح وأثره في الحياة
content_en:
Al-Uthaymin concludes his explanation of the foundations of faith by surveying the transformative effects that correct iman produces in the believer's life. This chapter is practical and pastoral in tone — the Sheikh moves from theological exposition to lived application, showing that the pillars of iman are not abstract intellectual positions but living convictions that reshape a person's entire orientation.

The first and most comprehensive fruit of correct iman is ikhlas — sincerity in worship. When a person truly believes in tawhid al-uluhiyyah, he directs all his acts of devotion exclusively to Allah and is liberated from the servitude of human beings, cultural expectations, and material desires. Al-Uthaymin notes that this is the deepest form of human freedom: the believer bows to no one but his Creator.

A second major fruit is tranquility (tuma'ninah) of the heart. The Quran states: 'Verily, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find rest' (13:28). The person with correct iman in Allah's rububiyyah knows that all provision, all protection, and all outcomes are in Allah's hands. He exerts full effort in pursuing his goals but does not experience the crushing anxiety of one who believes he must control everything himself. This tranquility is especially evident in how the believer faces death, illness, and loss: faith in the Last Day transforms these experiences from meaningless suffering into tests with eternal significance.

Third, correct iman produces moral uprightness. The person who believes that the recording angels document every deed, that the scales of justice will be set on the Day of Judgment, and that Allah sees all hidden actions will behave honestly even when no human observer is present. The Quran repeatedly links iman to righteous deeds (al-'amal as-salih), indicating that authentic inner conviction naturally produces ethical behavior.

Fourth, al-Uthaymin discusses how iman builds community. Shared belief in Allah, in the Prophet, peace be upon him, and in the Last Day creates the foundation of the Muslim ummah. The Prophet said: 'None of you truly believes until he loves for his brother what he loves for himself.' This fraternal love, which transcends tribal, racial, and national boundaries, flows from a common iman.

The Sheikh also addresses how correct iman protects against deviations. A person grounded in the six pillars — and especially in correct tawhid — will recognize innovations in religion (bid'ah) for what they are, because he understands that acts of worship must be commanded by Allah or His messenger. He will not be deceived by charismatic individuals who invite to deviance, because his anchor is the Quran and the authentic Sunnah.

The chapter concludes with a reminder of the obligation to continue studying and deepening one's iman throughout life. Al-Uthaymin encourages students to move from this introductory text to the more detailed works of the scholars — the books of aqeedah by Ibn Taymiyyah, Ibn al-Qayyim, and the major works of the classical scholars — always with the goal of increasing not merely in knowledge but in true conviction and fear of Allah. Knowledge of iman, he teaches, is not a destination but a lifelong journey of drawing nearer to Allah.
